prompt:
Ticket from the developer-productivity rotation. Our monorepo builds with a shell script that rebuilds the world on every change; people have started passing binaries around in chat to dodge it, which is how we shipped a stale one last week. I want the core of a real build tool as a Go library — in-process and testable: build steps are plain Go callbacks, not subprocesses, and there's no CLI yet. Module go-buildgraph, package buildgraph at the repo root, stdlib only, gofmt-clean, `go test -race ./...` green against the tests we already wrote (graph_test.go, exec_test.go — hands off both). Split the implementation across exactly these six files: manifest.go (build.json decoding + validation), workspace.go (tree walk, labels, Workspace/Target types, OutPath), graph.go (dep resolution, cycle detection, deterministic topological order), state.go (content hashing + the on-disk build state), plan.go (dry-run plans + FormatPlan), exec.go (Executor, the bounded worker pool, Execute + Report).

LOADING. LoadWorkspace(root string) (*Workspace, error). Every build.json under root is a package manifest; its package name is the manifest's directory relative to root with forward slashes, "" for the root itself. The walk skips root's out/ directory entirely — that's our output tree, never source. Manifest shape: {"targets": [{"name", "action", "inputs", "deps"}]}, decoded strictly: an unknown field anywhere is an error whose text includes "unknown field", and every manifest problem (bad JSON included) is reported prefixed with the manifest's root-relative path. Per-target rules, each rejected with an error quoting the offender: name non-empty with no ':' or '/'; no duplicate names within a manifest; action required; inputs are package-relative source files that may not be absolute or climb out via '..' (the word input should appear in that error). A target's label is "<package>:<name>" — root targets read ":name". deps come in three spellings: "name" (same package), ":name" (root package), "pkg/path:name" (fully qualified); Target.Deps carries them canonicalized to full labels while Target.Inputs stays byte-for-byte as declared. Workspace API: Targets() []string (every label, sorted), Target(label) (*Target, bool) with Name/Action/Inputs/Deps exposed, and OutPath(label) string = <root>/out/<package>/<name>. After parsing, a dep that names no known target fails the load with BOTH labels in the error; a dependency cycle fails it with exactly this shape: "dependency cycle: a:one -> b:two -> c:three -> a:one" — members in edge order, first label repeated at the end, starting from the cycle's lexicographically smallest member regardless of where detection walked in (a self-loop reads "dependency cycle: d:d -> d:d").

ACTIONS. type ActionFunc func(*TaskContext) error. TaskContext carries Label, Inputs (absolute paths, declaration order), DepOutputs (the deps' output paths sorted by dep label), and OutPath (absolute; the action must create this file). NewExecutor(ws, Options{Workers int, Actions map[string]ActionFunc}) validates eagerly: Workers < 1 errors mentioning workers; a target whose action isn't registered errors naming that target and the action name, first offender in sorted label order.

PLAN. Plan(label) ([]Step, error), Step{Label string; Run bool}: what Execute would do, in execution order — dependencies before dependents, lexicographic among ties (the classic diamond plans as lib:base, lib:left, lib:right, app:bin). Planning is read-only: no actions run, nothing is written, out/ must not even get created. It errors on an unknown label (named) and on a missing input file anywhere in the cone (path in the error). Plan-time dirtiness is pessimistic: a target is Run if it has no state record, or its recorded input hashes / dep-output hashes / own-output hash no longer match the disk, or ANY dependency is itself planned to run — Plan does not try to predict early cutoff. FormatPlan(steps) string: one line per step, "run <label>" or "skip <label>", each newline-terminated.

EXECUTE. Execute(label) (*Report, error) brings the label's dependency cone up to date, running at most Workers actions in flight — and genuinely fanning out: with Workers=2 and a four-wide independent layer, two actions run simultaneously, and nowhere may in-flight exceed Workers. A diamond's shared dependency builds once per call. Freshness is content hashing, never mtimes — rewriting a file with identical bytes changes nothing. A target is up to date when its state record matches the current input hashes and dep-output hashes and its own output file still carries the recorded hash; anything else means mkdir the output's parent, run the action, and verify it wrote OutPath (missing output: error naming the target and mentioning the output). Early cutoff is the whole point: dependents compare against the dep's FRESH post-run output hash, so a rebuild that reproduces byte-identical output stops the wave right there — Plan may overestimate, Execute must not. Deleting an output file reruns only its own target. State lives at out/state.json as JSON; missing or unparseable state means a full rebuild, never an error; state is saved at the end of EVERY Execute, failed ones included, so a rerun after fixing a flaky step skips the survivors. Report{Ran, UpToDate []string}, both sorted, and the report pointer is non-nil even when an error comes back (reflecting whatever finished first). A missing input file errors naming the target and the path, with nothing marked Ran. An action error is returned wrapped — errors.Is must reach the original — prefixed with the failing target's label; nothing downstream of a failure runs and no new work starts once something has failed. Executing an unknown label errors naming it. No sleeps, no wall-clock, no environment reads: byte-identical trees must make byte-identical decisions.
